insights: Product Management (26.4%), Customer Support (23.0%), and Finance (18.2%) collectively account for 67.6% of total departmental spending, indicating a highly concentrated allocation across just three departments with clear operational or strategic priority.

Services is the dominant expense category across all seven departments (38.8% of total spend) and the top category in every department — yet overall category concentration remains low (HHI=0.2887), reflecting diversified spending behavior outside this single high-volume category.

Only 22.1% of expense records have reached 'Processed' status, while 55.8% remain in active workflow states (Submitted + Pending), signaling severe throughput constraints and a systemic bottleneck in the expense processing pipeline.

Valid processing times are extremely long — median 79 days, with 78.6% exceeding 30 days — but show a strong decreasing trend (94.4% reduction from Jan to Oct 2024), suggesting recent process interventions are having material impact.

The 39% prevalence of negative processing_time_hours is a systematic data quality issue concentrated in non-Processed states (50.5% in Submitted, 53.2% in Pending, 45.8% in Declined), with no identifiable driver across amount, category, type, or user — implying flawed timestamp logic rather than behavioral patterns.

IT is the only department exhibiting strong category specialization (53.7% in Services), whereas four departments maintain balanced allocations (no category >40%), revealing divergent internal cost governance models despite Services being universally dominant.
summary: Departmental spending is heavily concentrated in Product Management, Customer Support, and Finance — together driving two-thirds of total expenses — while Services dominates category-level spend across all departments, though overall category distribution remains structurally diversified. Expense processing efficiency is critically low: less than a quarter of records are fully processed, over half remain in active workflow states, and median cycle time exceeds 79 days — yet a steep 94.4% improvement in processing speed from January to October signals effective recent interventions. The widespread occurrence of negative processing times (39% of all records) is a persistent data integrity issue tied to non-Processed states, with no discernible business pattern explaining it, pointing to upstream system timestamp misalignment rather than operational behavior. Next focus should prioritize root-cause investigation of the negative time anomaly in source systems and validation of whether the observed processing-time improvement is sustainable across higher-volume departments.
